elements.label: Attribute
elements.description: Attributes are semanti tags

elements.label: Object Type
elements.description: Types of information

elements.label: Presenter
elements.description: Someone giving a presentation at an event

elements.label: Session
elements.description: Sessions within a main event where presenters get to share their expertise

elements.label: Resource
elements.description: Something that can be used to solve a problem

elements.label: Project
elements.description: An time-bound idea or initiative of any stage

elements.label: Location
elements.description: Locations are countries, cities or other areas of geographical space.

elements.label: Sponsor
elements.description: A person or organization sponsoring an event or group

elements.label: Organization
elements.description: An established entity such as a university or company

elements.label: Track
elements.description: A topical track at a conference or event

elements.label: FAQ
elements.description: Commonly asked questions with corresponding answers

elements.label: Member
elements.description: People who are verified members of a group or event

elements.label: Post
elements.description: A social post that could include photos and other features like polls, etc.

elements.label: Video
elements.description: Recorded visual media

elements.label: Sector
elements.description: Main sectors of society

elements.label: Attendee
elements.description: People who are verified attendees of an event

elements.label: Sectors
elements.description: A sector in the catalist.network is a main domain of society and always comes in Sets.  For example, education, governance, and spirituality are all sectors in the Set called Wheel of Co-Creation.

elements.summary: Sun Li is a decentralized systems architect and digital commons researcher from Beijing, now based in San Francisco, working on the technical infrastructure for community self-governance. As a senior engineer at Hylo, she designs federated protocols for knowledge-sharing that prioritize community data sovereignty over platform extraction.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: What would it look like if the communities most harmed by extractive platforms were the designers of the alternatives?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Deep technical knowledge in federated protocol design, community data governance architecture, and engineering tools that center community sovereignty. Code reviews, architecture consulting, and bridge-building between technical and community practitioners.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Communities actively stewarding their own data infrastructure who want technical support. Movement technologists interested in collaborating on federated protocols for knowledge commons.
elements.label: Sun Li

elements.summary: Two-thirds of an octopus's neurons are in its arms, each capable of independent action. What does this architecture of distributed cognition suggest for how we think about intelligence, decision-making, and organizational design? A fast-paced, mind-bending talk connecting marine biology to governance theory.
elements.label: The Octopus Problem: Distributed Cognition and What It Teaches Us

elements.summary: Botanist and Citizen Potawatomi Nation member Robin Wall Kimmerer's essays weaving Indigenous teachings with the practice of Western science. Required reading for the Indigenous & Ancestral Knowledge track and a starting point for many of the festival's themes.
elements.label: Robin Wall Kimmerer — Braiding Sweetgrass

elements.summary: Indigenous communities around the world are asserting sovereignty over data about their people, lands, and cultural heritage — and designing protocols and platforms grounded in indigenous values rather than Silicon Valley logic. This talk by a Māori technologist and data sovereignty researcher offers an introduction to the emerging field of indigenous data governance.
elements.label: Sacred Technology: Indigenous Data Sovereignty and Protocol

elements.summary: Repair cafés — community spaces where people bring broken objects to fix together — function as local metabolic organs, extending the life of materials and rebuilding practical knowledge networks. This talk presents research on 500 repair cafés across 30 countries and proposes a framework for scaling repair as a form of ecological and social infrastructure.
elements.label: Repair Cafés as Community Metabolism

elements.summary: A 40-page zine compiling short field reports, sketches, and observations from practitioners working at the edges of living systems. Contributors were asked: 'What is one pattern you keep noticing?' The result is a diverse, idiosyncratic compendium of systems intelligence from the ground up.
elements.label: Pattern Notes: Field Observations from the Edges of Systems

elements.summary: A 52-page illustrated field guide to the living systems of the Willamette Valley and the Portland area — the watershed, soils, native species, indigenous history, and current ecological challenges. Designed for people who live in the valley and want to know the land they inhabit.
elements.label: A Bioregional Field Guide to the Willamette Valley

elements.summary: A curated selection of excerpts — scientific papers, essays, poems, and indigenous knowledge texts — exploring the intelligence of fungal networks and their implications for how we think about organization, communication, and community. Accompanied by original illustrations and reflection prompts.
elements.label: The Mycelium Reader: Key Texts on Fungal Intelligence and Network Thinking

elements.summary: A 64-page atlas of 50 cooperative and commons experiments from 30 countries — from worker co-ops and community land trusts to open-source seed networks and commons-based digital platforms. Each entry includes a map, a photo, a brief description, and contact information.
elements.label: Cooperative Atlas: 50 Experiments in Shared Life

elements.summary: A 28-page polemical zine arguing that the work of transformation requires first attending to what needs to die — and that our cultural inability to sit with breakdown is the primary obstacle to regeneration. Written by the Emergence Collective and illustrated with botanical prints.
elements.label: A Composting Manifesto: On Breakdown, Transformation, and What Remains

elements.summary: Primary virtual room broadcasting the shared opening, closing ceremony, and rotating keynote feeds from each in-person track. Captioned live, with a parallel chat moderated by the Emergence Collective stewards.
elements.label: Virtual Main Room

elements.summary: Secondary stream carrying the Systems Thinking & Design Science talks, plus virtual-only panels not represented on the main stream. Open chat and Q&A queue.
elements.label: Virtual Room 2

elements.address: 551 N Pleasant St, Amherst, MA 01003, USA
elements.summary: Six-story timber-and-glass design building with a tiered commons that doubles as a 250-seat lecture amphitheater. Hosts the Systems Thinking & Design Science track: keynotes, panels, and case studies on cooperative economics, governance futures, and planetary cooperation.
elements.label: John W. Olver Design Building — Commons & Studio Spaces

elements.summary: Cluster of small breakout rooms used for hybrid workshops: somatic dyads, cooperative-economics scenario play, and regenerative-design crit groups. Each room caps at ~25 participants; rotated by session.
elements.label: Virtual Distributed Breakout Rooms

elements.summary: The 600-seat flagship performance hall in the Randolph W. Bromery Center for the Arts. Hosts the Living Art track: performances, choreographic work, sound bath openings, and the festival-wide closing ceremony. Sprung floor, full lighting rig, balcony seating.
elements.label: Frederick C. Tillis Performance Hall

elements.address: Studio Arts Bldg, 110 Thatcher Rd, Amherst, MA 01003, USA
elements.summary: Working studios and a ground-floor gallery transformed into immersive installation rooms for the day. Hosts the Sacred Geometry & Biomimicry track: light-and-pattern installations, mycelium prototyping, generative drawing labs, and printmaking workshops.
elements.label: Studio Arts Building (SAB) — Gallery & Studios

elements.summary: Outdoor twin-venue: the Olver rooftop courtyard for somatic mornings and the Campus Pond lawn for regenerative-design demos and biomimicry walks. Hosts the Regenerative & Somatic track. Weather contingency: tented overflow on the Olver portico.
elements.label: Olver Design Building Rooftop Courtyard / Campus Pond Lawn

elements.summary: Systems Design Lab is an applied research studio and learning community for practitioners building regenerative futures. Through intensive design sprints, collaborative experiments, and a year-round fellowship program, the Lab supports the next generation of systems-literate designers and changemakers working at the edge of ecology, technology, and social transformation.
elements.label: Systems Design Lab

elements.summary: Yuki Tanaka is a biomimicry designer and educator based in Kyoto whose practice translates principles from living systems into architecture, product design, and organizational form. He is a founding faculty member of the Japan Biomimicry Institute and collaborates with traditional craftspeople to bridge ancient and emergent design intelligences.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: When we say a building "breathes like a forest," are we reaching for poetry or for a genuinely different design intelligence — and does that difference matter?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Translation expertise between biological principles and design applications in architecture and product design. Faculty access through the Japan Biomimicry Institute. Bridge-building between traditional Japanese craft and contemporary biomimetic design intelligence.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Biologists willing to enter into genuine creative collaboration with designers — not as consultants but as co-creators. Traditional craftspeople from any culture interested in exploring resonances between ancient making practices and living systems.
elements.label: Yuki Tanaka

elements.summary: Nadia Schulz is a complexity scientist and systems educator whose work bridges academia and social movements. Based in Berlin, she consults with NGOs and municipalities on applying complexity theory to governance challenges. Her book, 'The Grammar of Emergence,' is used in university courses across Europe and Latin America.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Social movements have always operated as complex adaptive systems — how do we give them language to understand their own dynamics without over-rationalizing their aliveness?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Expertise applying complexity theory to social systems and governance. Teaching and facilitation across academic and activist contexts. European NGO and policy networks for distribution of applied complexity thinking.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Social movements and community organizers interested in working with complexity concepts. Policy makers genuinely curious about designing for emergence rather than control.
elements.label: Nadia Schulz

elements.summary: Ecomakers Collective connects innovators working at the intersection of biomimicry, ecological design, indigenous knowledge, and social transformation. Through their annual gathering and a year-round media network, Ecomakers amplifies practitioners who are reweaving humanity's relationship with the living world — from regenerative agriculture and nature-inspired technology to community-scale food systems and bioregional governance.
elements.label: Ecomakers Collective

elements.summary: The Geodesic Design Institute advances whole-systems design as a response to humanity's interconnected challenges. Inspired by principles of synergetic thinking and elegant resource efficiency, GDI supports researchers, designers, and educators developing integrated solutions that do more with less. Their annual design challenge attracts teams from over 60 countries working on proposals for regenerative infrastructure.
elements.label: Geodesic Design Institute

elements.summary: James Whitehorse is a member of the Cherokee Nation and a systems thinker whose work integrates indigenous ecological knowledge with contemporary complexity science. As a senior fellow at the Buckminster Fuller Institute, he facilitates cross-cultural design processes and has developed curricula that center indigenous science in higher education.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Western science is beginning to discover what indigenous knowledge has always known. Will it be humble enough to cite its sources?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Cross-cultural design process facilitation developed over twenty years at the BFI. Curricula that center indigenous science as epistemology, not just case study. A Cherokee perspective on systems thinking and complexity that challenges Western framings.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Academic institutions genuinely committed to decolonizing their science curricula — not just adding an indigenous module, but restructuring the epistemological foundations of the whole enterprise.
elements.label: James Whitehorse

elements.summary: Lena Moreau is a choreographer and somatic movement researcher based in Paris whose work explores the body as a site of ecological intelligence. Her recent piece, 'Mycorrhizal Movements,' performed internationally at venues including the Centre Pompidou and the Walker Art Center, renders underground fungal communication visible through collective human movement.
What themes best represent your work?: 3. Good health and well-being for people
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Mycorrhizal communication happens in time scales humans can barely perceive. What would it mean to practice at that pace — to make work that is slower than our attention spans?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: The Mycorrhizal Movements choreographic methodology and its translation into organizational and educational contexts. Performance connections at the Centre Pompidou and Walker Art Center. A practice for making invisible ecological processes perceptible through collective movement.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Scientists working in mycology or fungal ecology genuinely interested in artistic collaboration. Venues and institutions willing to program work that requires audience patience and institutional slowness.
elements.label: Lena Moreau

elements.address: 3130 SE Hawthorne Blvd, Portland, OR 97214, USA
elements.summary: Equinox Arts Center is a multidisciplinary arts venue committed to cultural transformation and deep community rootedness. Hosting over 250 events annually, Equinox serves as a living laboratory where artists, activists, and thinkers converge to experiment with new forms of collective expression and civic imagination. The Center prioritizes work that blurs boundaries between art, ecology, and social justice.
elements.label: Equinox Arts Center

elements.address: São Paulo, State of São Paulo, Brazil
elements.summary: Rafael Santos is a regenerative economics researcher and cooperative developer based in São Paulo. As a senior researcher at the New Economy Coalition, he studies solidarity economy networks in Brazil and their relationship to global commons movements. His doctoral dissertation mapped 3,000 solidarity economy enterprises across Latin America.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Latin America's solidarity economy is the world's largest living laboratory of economic alternatives. Why is it still treated as a footnote in global economic discourse?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: The most comprehensive map of solidarity economy enterprises in Latin America. New Economy Coalition research connections. Deep knowledge of how cooperative networks function at scale across diverse cultural and political contexts.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Policy makers willing to take solidarity economy research seriously as evidence of viable economic alternatives. Journalists and communicators who can help bring this body of work to audiences that will never read an academic paper.
elements.label: Rafael Santos

elements.summary: The Emergence Institute is a global action-research community working to catalyze social and institutional transformation through practices of deep listening, collective sensing, and prototyping from the future. Drawing on the science of attention and the wisdom of complex adaptive systems, the Institute supports leaders and communities navigating profound uncertainty — with programs active in over 30 countries across six continents.
elements.label: The Emergence Institute

elements.summary: Elena Volkov is a systems educator and facilitator at the Presencing Institute whose work applies Theory U to organizational transformation in post-Soviet contexts. Born in Moscow, she now splits her time between Toronto and a rural cooperative farm she helped found in Ontario, where she runs immersive leadership programs in relationship with the land.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: How do we help organizations that want to change but are terrified of what they'll find when they stop moving long enough to sense?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Deep expertise in Theory U and presencing methodologies. Facilitation experience with organizations in complex transitions. Connection to the global Presencing Institute network and its research on systems transformation.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Organizations in post-Soviet contexts ready to experiment with new leadership models. Rural land-based communities in Eastern Europe interested in collaborative design of economic structures that work with rather than against the land.
elements.label: Elena Volkov

elements.summary: Deep Patterns Lab is a research and strategy studio working on the underlying structures of change — the governance frameworks, institutional models, and cultural operating systems that either enable or obstruct systemic transformation. The Lab combines rigorous social science with speculative design to prototype new forms of collective organization and public infrastructure.
elements.label: Deep Patterns Lab

elements.summary: Marcus Rivera is an urban ecologist and designer based in Mexico City whose work reframes cities as living ecosystems. Through his studio Tejido Urbano, he works with informal settlements to co-design green infrastructure that addresses both ecological and social vulnerabilities. His TED talk 'Cities that Breathe' has been viewed over 4 million times.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: The informal settlement is not a failed city — it is a living system. How do we design policies that amplify its intelligence rather than replace it with ours?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Studio Tejido Urbano's methodology for co-designing green infrastructure with informal settlement communities. Four million TED audience connections as a platform for ideas. Bridge-building between urban ecology science and community design practice.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Municipal governments willing to invest in community-led ecological infrastructure. Researchers in urban ecology and informal settlement governance interested in genuine design collaboration.
elements.label: Marcus Rivera

elements.summary: The New Futures Coalition is a network of over 180 organizations building a democratic, equitable, and regenerative economy. NFC connects cooperatives, community land trusts, worker organizations, and social enterprises to share strategies, resources, and political power. Their annual convergence and shared learning infrastructure help member organizations grow in depth and solidarity.
elements.label: New Futures Coalition

elements.summary: Priya Krishnamurthy is a transition designer whose practice focuses on the design of systemic interventions that shift sociotechnical systems toward more just and sustainable futures. Based between Bangalore and London, she teaches at the Royal College of Art and has led projects for the UN Development Programme and multiple national governments.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: How do we design for transitions that are dignified — that hold people in the disruption rather than optimizing them through it?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Methodologies for systemic intervention design developed through work with the UN and national governments. Teaching capacity in transition design at the Royal College of Art. Frameworks for mapping leverage points in sociotechnical systems.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Practitioners working at the community level whose on-the-ground experience can push back on and enrich academic frameworks. Organizations wanting to experiment with participatory design in governance transitions.
elements.label: Priya Krishnamurthy

elements.summary: Gaia Collective builds open-source tools and practices for regenerative communities. Their platforms support resource mapping, mutual aid coordination, and collective knowledge-building — grounded in the belief that technology can serve as mycelium for human solidarity. Gaia Collective works at the intersection of digital commons, cooperative governance, and ecological design.
elements.label: Gaia Collective

elements.summary: Zenith Institute is a learning sanctuary dedicated to personal and collective transformation. Through residential workshops, seasonal gatherings, and an expanding online community, Zenith brings together teachers, practitioners, and seekers across wellness, ecology, and social renewal. Their flagship campus hosts over 350 programs annually, drawing participants from across North America and beyond.
What themes best represent your work?: 3. Good health and well-being for people
elements.label: Zenith Institute

elements.summary: Amara is a systems designer and urban futurist at Deep Pattern Labs working on the governance infrastructure of cities transitioning toward regenerative urbanism. Originally from Accra, she brings a pan-African development lens to her work on polycentric governance and commons-based city design. Her recent project, 'Distributed Futures,' mapped governance experiments across 12 cities in the Global South.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: The governance experiments I study in the Global South are more sophisticated than anything happening in Western policy labs — when does the direction of learning reverse?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Deep Pattern Labs' methodology for governance infrastructure design. Research from "Distributed Futures" across 12 cities. A pan-African development lens on commons-based city design that centers the Global South as innovator.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: City officials and urban planners willing to move beyond consultation toward genuine co-governance. Researchers mapping polycentric governance experiments who want a collaborator thinking from the African continent.
elements.label: Amara Osei-Bonsu

elements.summary: Kwame Asante is a spoken word poet and community educator from Accra, now based in Brooklyn, whose work weaves together Akan oral traditions, contemporary social theory, and speculative futures. His collection 'The Forest Floor Speaks' won the African Poetry Award in 2024. He teaches at The New School and leads workshops on voice, memory, and collective healing.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Language is architecture. What kind of world does the language we are currently using build — and is it a world worth living in?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: A practice of spoken word rooted in Akan oral traditions and speculative futures. Workshop facilitation on voice, memory, and collective storytelling. The African Poetry Award recognition and the network that comes with it.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Educators and community organizers who want to use oral traditions as living tools for community building and political imagination. Publishers and platforms interested in African speculative poetry as a form of world-making.
elements.label: Kwame Asante

elements.summary: Emergence Network is a distributed community of practitioners, artists, and systems thinkers dedicated to transformative learning and collective sense-making. Founded in 2019, the Network produces convenings, learning experiences, and collaborative projects at the edges of art, ecology, and social change — with a particular focus on practices that cultivate both inner and outer transformation.
elements.label: Emergence Network

elements.summary: Saoirse McCarthy is a choral director and community musician from Dublin whose work uses collective singing as a form of social infrastructure. She founded the Resonance Choir Collective, a network of 40 community choirs in Ireland and the UK, and consults internationally on using music as a tool for social cohesion and post-conflict reconciliation.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: What is the minimum viable infrastructure for collective singing to become social infrastructure — and who gets to decide what counts as enough?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: A proven methodology for establishing self-sustaining community choirs in under-resourced contexts. Experience using music in post-conflict reconciliation across Ireland and the UK. A network of 40 community choirs as a peer learning community.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Communities recovering from collective trauma who might benefit from choir as social infrastructure. Researchers interested in documenting the long-term social effects of participatory music practice.
elements.label: Saoirse McCarthy

elements.summary: Tomás Fernández is a visual and installation artist from Buenos Aires whose large-scale works explore the tension between natural systems and human infrastructure. Working with salvaged industrial materials, living plants, and biosensors, his installations function as hybrid ecosystems that change over time in response to environmental conditions.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: My installations are alive — the plants grow, the sensors shift. At what point does the work begin making its own decisions, and am I still the artist?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Technical expertise in bioart and living installation — biosensors, hydroponic systems, salvaged industrial materials placed in ecological tension. Studio space in Buenos Aires for collaborative projects that need time and mess.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Scientists and ecologists whose data I can give material form. Institutions willing to host living installations that change over time and require ongoing care rather than passive display.
elements.label: Tomás Fernández

elements.summary: Amir Hassan is a composer and musicologist from Cairo whose work bridges classical Arabic maqam traditions and contemporary electronic music. He is the founder of Resonant Earth, an international collaboration of musicians using sound as a medium for cross-cultural dialogue and ecological awareness. His compositions incorporate field recordings from threatened ecosystems worldwide.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: The Arabic maqam system maps emotional states onto specific microtonal intervals with extraordinary precision. What if governance and healing were as precise about the inner life as music has always been?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Deep knowledge of classical Arabic maqam traditions and contemporary electronic composition. Resonant Earth's network of international musicians working with sound as ecological awareness. Field recordings from threatened ecosystems as shared compositional material.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Scientists and ecologists whose data I can translate into sound. Cultural institutions genuinely interested in cross-cultural musical dialogue that centers ecological awareness rather than exoticizing difference.
elements.label: Amir Hassan

elements.summary: Valentina Cruz is a choreographer and somatic practitioner from Santiago whose work explores movement as a form of political and ecological testimony. Trained in contemporary dance and indigenous Mapuche movement practices, she creates performances that are simultaneously aesthetic objects and acts of cultural continuity. She is founding director of the dance collective Raíz Viva.
What themes best represent your work?: 3. Good health and well-being for people
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: My body holds the memory of colonial violence and the intelligence of ancestral resistance simultaneously. How do I teach from both without separating them into something manageable?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: A choreographic practice rooted in contemporary dance and Mapuche movement traditions. Raíz Viva's collective approach to performance as cultural continuity and political testimony. Work that refuses the separation of the aesthetic from the political.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Venues and festivals genuinely interested in presenting work that is simultaneously aesthetic experience and political act. Researchers in somatic practice and decolonial performance willing to think with practitioners rather than about them.
elements.label: Valentina Cruz

elements.summary: Oluwaseun Adeyemi is a filmmaker and visual artist from Lagos whose documentary practice centers African perspectives on land, memory, and transformation. His film 'The Living Archive' won the Grand Jury Prize at the Pan African Film Festival and has been screened at over 60 festivals worldwide. He is founder of the Lagos Open Cinema project.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Every archive is a political act. Whose lives are being archived, by whom, and for whose future — and what gets lost in the translation from living to recorded?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: A documentary practice that centers African voices on land, memory, and transformation. The Lagos Open Cinema project's community screening model. Grand Jury Prize credibility and the networks that come with it.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Communities wanting to document their own living archives before they are lost to time or displacement. Foundations willing to fund African cinema as a form of knowledge sovereignty rather than just cultural expression.
elements.label: Oluwaseun Adeyemi

elements.summary: Kofi Mensah is a traditional performer, griot, and cultural educator from Kumasi, Ghana, who carries knowledge of Akan storytelling, drumming, and ancestral communication traditions. He travels internationally to share these living knowledge systems and their relevance to contemporary questions of governance, ecology, and collective memory.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: A griot is a living archive. What happens to a community that loses its living archives — and can we feel that loss before it becomes irreversible?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Living knowledge of Akan storytelling, drumming, and ancestral communication that no text can fully hold. International teaching experience. The irreplaceable perspective of a practitioner who carries knowledge that must be transmitted in person.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Institutions committed to honoring and supporting living knowledge transmission on its own terms — not documentation or digitization, but the continuation of the practice itself across generations.
elements.label: Kofi Mensah

elements.summary: Hana Nakamura is a movement practitioner and somatic educator from Osaka who integrates Butoh, contact improvisation, and Zen Buddhist practice in her work with individuals, groups, and organizations. Her method, Embodied Field Intelligence, is practiced in 15 countries and is increasingly applied in leadership development and trauma-informed community organizing.
What themes best represent your work?: 3. Good health and well-being for people
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Contact improvisation requires two bodies to agree to share weight without knowing what will happen next. Can communities learn to do that at scale?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Embodied Field Intelligence methodology practiced in 15 countries. Application of somatic practice to leadership development and trauma-informed community organizing. An integration of Butoh, contact improvisation, and Zen practice that has proven useful far beyond dance.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Organizations that want to embody their values rather than merely articulate them. Communities dealing with collective transition or conflict who want a somatic approach rather than another strategic planning process.
elements.label: Hana Nakamura

elements.summary: Yael Mizrahi is a sound artist and acoustic ecologist based in Tel Aviv whose practice investigates the sonic life of ecosystems and the relationship between listening and attention. Her long-duration sound installations have been presented at the Venice Biennale, Documenta, and in forests, wetlands, and urban margins across four continents.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: We have lost the ability to sit with long sound — with duration, with silence, with a place that has not been designed. What are we losing along with it?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Long-duration sound installation practice presented at the Venice Biennale, Documenta, and in forests and wetlands worldwide. Techniques for deep listening and acoustic ecology fieldwork. Experience in ecologically and politically contested landscapes.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Ecologists, activists, and organizers interested in acoustic environments as sites of political and ecological attention. Curators willing to program work that requires unusual patience from audiences and institutions.
elements.label: Yael Mizrahi

elements.summary: Clara Bjørnstad is a Norwegian textile artist and ecologist whose work uses traditional Nordic weaving techniques to map and make visible the patterns of ecosystems under climate stress. Her large-scale woven landscapes have been exhibited in natural history museums and contemporary art institutions across Scandinavia, Germany, and the UK.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: How can the act of making — of weaving, of doing with hands — become a form of ecological documentation that outlives any research paper?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Expertise in using weaving and traditional textile practices as a medium for visualizing complex ecological data. I can help collaborators make the invisible visible — the patterns, stresses, and rhythms of living systems — through material craft.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Ecologists and scientists curious about embodied, craft-based approaches to communicating their research. Residency hosts in places of ecological significance.
elements.label: Clara Bjørnstad

elements.summary: Mei Lin is a Shanghai-born interdisciplinary artist now based in Vancouver whose practice spans performance, installation, and community ritual. Working with materials sourced from urban and natural environments, she creates participatory experiences that invite reflection on belonging, displacement, and the permeability of boundaries between human and non-human worlds.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: What does it mean to create belonging in a body that has crossed so many boundaries — national, ecological, cultural — that it carries all of them simultaneously?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: A practice of participatory community ritual that requires no shared language — only presence and willingness. Materials-based installation methods that invite reflection on the permeability of boundaries between human and non-human worlds.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Site-specific residencies in places of active ecological and social tension. Collaborators from indigenous and diasporic communities who work with ceremony as a form of art and political action.
elements.label: Mei Lin

elements.summary: Dr. Marcus Webb is a water systems ecologist and environmental justice researcher based in New Orleans who studies the relationship between hydrological systems and community resilience in climate-vulnerable coastal regions. His work integrates scientific hydrology with community-based participatory research and has been central to post-Katrina ecological restoration planning.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: The Mississippi Delta knows how to heal itself. The question is whether we have the political will to get out of its way and let it.
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Expertise in hydrological systems ecology and community-based participatory research. Connection to Gulf Coast communities and ecological restoration networks. A research framework that bridges scientific hydrology with environmental justice.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Community organizations in climate-vulnerable coastal regions wanting to integrate scientific mapping with indigenous and local ecological knowledge. Environmental justice advocates interested in equitable science partnerships.
elements.label: Dr. Marcus Webb

elements.summary: Amara Diallo is a cooperative housing organizer and architect from Dakar, now based in Montreal, whose work develops community land trusts and cooperative housing models in West African and Afro-diasporic communities. She is co-founder of the Maison Partagée collective and has organized over 400 units of cooperative housing in Montreal, Dakar, and Abidjan.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: We have housed 400 families cooperatively across three countries. The hardest part was never the architecture — it was helping people believe they deserved stable, beautiful homes.
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Expertise in community land trust and cooperative housing development across contexts. Deep connections in Dakar, Montreal, and Abidjan cooperative housing movements. Architecture practice that centers community ownership as both goal and process.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Philanthropic and public investment in community-controlled housing outside the market. Communities in Africa and its diaspora interested in cooperative housing models that work across cultural contexts.
elements.label: Amara Diallo

elements.summary: Dr. Ravi Kapoor is a complexity scientist at the Santa Fe Institute whose research investigates the conditions under which self-organizing systems produce emergent intelligence. Born in Mumbai, he studied at IIT Delhi and Cambridge and now applies complexity theory to questions of urban resilience, collective decision-making, and institutional evolution.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Emergence is a word we use when a system surprises us. Are we surprised by emergence because we don't understand systems — or because we're too invested in control to let them work?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Complexity science frameworks for understanding emergence, self-organization, and institutional evolution developed at the Santa Fe Institute. Ability to make complexity accessible to non-specialist audiences. Research connections across complexity science globally.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Urban planners, governance practitioners, and social movements willing to think seriously with complexity science. Funders willing to support long-duration, non-linear research that cannot promise outcomes in advance.
elements.label: Dr. Ravi Kapoor

elements.summary: Dr. Sarah Windmere is a Portland-based ecologist and bioregional researcher whose work maps living systems across the Pacific Northwest with the goal of restoring watershed integrity at scale. A Bioneers fellow and co-founder of the Cascadia Bioregional Institute, she bridges scientific mapping with indigenous land knowledge and community-based stewardship.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: What if the Pacific Northwest could name itself? What governance structures would this bioregion choose — and what would it need from us to actually thrive?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Pacific Northwest bioregional research and watershed mapping. Co-founder access to the Cascadia Bioregional Institute's network. A methodology for bridging scientific mapping with indigenous land knowledge and community-based stewardship.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Indigenous land stewards in the Pacific Northwest interested in collaborative bioregional research on their own terms. Policy makers curious about bioregional governance as an alternative to administrative boundaries that ignore ecological reality.
elements.label: Dr. Sarah Windmere

elements.summary: Dr. Ingrid Halvorsen is a marine biologist and regenerative ocean advocate based in Bergen whose research examines the ecological intelligence of marine ecosystems and their potential as models for human governance and design. Her book 'The Ocean Thinks' has been translated into 11 languages and won the Norwegian Environmental Literature Prize.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: The ocean governed itself far more successfully before we arrived. At what point does our "management" of marine ecosystems become more harmful than our leaving them alone?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Marine ecosystem intelligence research translated for governance and design audiences. "The Ocean Thinks" network across 11 language communities. Scientific grounding for biomimetic ocean-inspired governance models that are more than metaphor.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Governance designers and complexity scientists seriously interested in ocean systems as models for human organization. Artists and communicators who can help translate marine science for audiences beyond the academy.
elements.label: Dr. Ingrid Halvorsen

elements.summary: Dr. Kenji Yamamoto is a quantum biologist at the University of Tokyo who studies how biological systems exploit quantum phenomena — coherence, entanglement, tunneling — for navigation, photosynthesis, and cognition. His work invites radical rethinking of the boundary between living and non-living, and the intelligence embedded in material processes.
What themes best represent your work?: 3. Good health and well-being for people
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Quantum coherence in biological systems suggests that life is stranger and more entangled than any metaphor we have built for it. How do we govern from that understanding?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Research access to quantum biology's frontier — photosynthesis, navigation, and cognition at the quantum scale. The conceptual tools to radically rethink the boundary between living and non-living, and the intelligence embedded in material processes.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Philosophers, artists, and governance thinkers willing to seriously engage with what quantum biology implies for how we understand intelligence, agency, and aliveness in systems of all kinds.
elements.label: Dr. Kenji Yamamoto

elements.summary: Dr. Ana Lima is a permaculture researcher and agroecologist at the University of Lisbon whose work investigates the social and ecological conditions under which traditional farming communities maintain soil biodiversity across generations. She has conducted field research across Portugal, Brazil, and Mozambique and is a founder of the European Regenerative Agriculture Network.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: The farmers I study carry more soil knowledge in their hands than in any laboratory — how do we build research institutions that are humble enough to learn from them?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Field research connections across Portugal, Brazil, and Mozambique. Scientific knowledge of soil biodiversity and traditional farming practices. Access to the European Regenerative Agriculture Network and its practitioner community.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Traditional farmers and knowledge holders willing to engage in co-research. Social scientists who can help design methodologies that honor oral and embodied agricultural knowledge as valid scientific sources.
elements.label: Dr. Ana Lima

elements.summary: Dr. Chisom Nwachukwu is a social scientist and commons researcher at the University of Lagos, now based in London, whose work investigates how communities in West Africa govern shared resources — land, water, knowledge — through customary institutional arrangements that predate and survive state failure.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: The commons never disappeared — it was criminalized. What would it take to name that clearly, and build from the evidence of what survived?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Deep research knowledge of West African commons governance traditions and their contemporary relevance. Academic publishing and policy translation capacity. Connections across West African scholarly and practitioner networks.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Practitioners in the solidarity economy willing to ground academic theory in living examples. Publishers and communicators interested in commons research that speaks to both policy audiences and practicing communities.
elements.label: Dr. Chisom Nwachukwu

elements.summary: Tenzin Dorje is a Buddhist economist and contemplative activist from Dharamsala whose work bridges Tibetan Buddhist philosophy with contemporary economic theory and cooperative practice. He founded the Jokhang Institute for Contemplative Economics and works with social enterprises across Asia and North America on applying insight meditation principles to organizational life.
What themes best represent your work?: 3. Good health and well-being for people
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Can we design economic structures that train the qualities of mind that regeneration requires — patience, equanimity, the willingness to work for the seventh generation?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Integration of contemplative practice and economic theory. Curriculum developed at the Jokhang Institute for Contemplative Economics. Experience designing meditation-based leadership programs for social enterprises across Asia and North America.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Economists and finance practitioners curious about how contemplative practice can transform the quality of economic decision-making. Communities experimenting with time-banking, gift economies, or other non-extractive models.
elements.label: Tenzin Dorje

elements.summary: Dr. Abebe Tadesse is a mycologist at Addis Ababa University whose research focuses on the role of fungal networks in forest regeneration across the Horn of Africa. A Bioneers fellow, he combines Western mycological science with indigenous Ethiopian knowledge of fungi to develop land restoration practices that work with traditional land stewards.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: The mycelial network has no center and no leader, yet it governs the entire forest. I have been studying this for twenty years and am still learning what it means.
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Mycological expertise and field research connections across the Horn of Africa. A bridge between Western mycological science and Ethiopian indigenous fungal knowledge. Land restoration methodologies developed in genuine partnership with traditional communities.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Indigenous communities across Africa interested in co-developing land restoration practices that honor their knowledge systems. Biomimicry designers wanting to ground their practice in African ecological intelligence rather than European or North American examples.
elements.label: Dr. Abebe Tadesse

elements.summary: Sophia Andersen is a Continuum Movement teacher and researcher from Copenhagen whose practice uses fluid, ocean-inspired movement to restore the body's inherent intelligence and self-regulating capacity. She has studied with Continuum founder Emilie Conrad and has extended the practice into ecological education and somatic approaches to climate grief.
What themes best represent your work?: 3. Good health and well-being for people
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Climate grief is a form of ecological knowledge — what would it look like to let bodies process what minds refuse to hold?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Deep training in Continuum Movement and its extension into ecological education. Experience developing somatic approaches to climate grief. Programs for re-inhabiting the body as an ecological instrument that can sense and respond to planetary change.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Environmental educators curious about somatic approaches to human-ecological relationships. Grief workers and therapists interested in training that bridges personal and planetary scales of loss and transformation.
elements.label: Sophia Andersen

elements.summary: Dr. Jolene Okafor is an educator and curriculum developer at the Buckminster Fuller Institute whose work designs learning environments that cultivate systems literacy and design thinking in young people. Originally from Lagos, she worked for a decade in Oxford developing open-source educational materials now used in 25 countries.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: We talk about systems literacy as though it's a skill — but what if it's a relationship? What does it take to fall in love with a system?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Open-source educational materials on systems thinking and design science, currently used in 25 countries. Curriculum development expertise for diverse cultural contexts. Deep connection to the Buckminster Fuller Institute's global network.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Educators in under-resourced contexts who want to adapt systems literacy curricula to their local realities. Funders supporting educational commons and open curriculum as a form of knowledge sovereignty.
elements.label: Dr. Jolene Okafor

elements.summary: Dayo Oyelaran is an African dance educator and cultural practitioner from Lagos, now based in Atlanta, who works with Yoruba and Pan-African movement traditions as living technologies for community healing, intergenerational connection, and political resistance. She is founding director of the Àṣà Dance Conservatory and consults with organizations on embodied approaches to diversity and belonging.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Yoruba movement traditions carry political theory, governance philosophy, and ecological knowledge — why does the West only want the performance?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Deep knowledge of Yoruba and Pan-African movement traditions as living technologies for community healing and political resistance. Experience consulting with organizations on embodied approaches to belonging and institutional transformation.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Organizations that want to move beyond performative diversity toward genuine embodied transformation. African diaspora communities rebuilding cultural practice who want a skilled and rooted collaborator.
elements.label: Dayo Oyelaran

elements.address: Rio de Janeiro, State of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
elements.summary: Carlos Eduardo Mendes is a capoeira master and movement educator from Rio de Janeiro whose practice bridges the martial art's Afro-Brazilian roots with contemporary applications in conflict transformation, embodied leadership, and ecological practice. He is founder of the Axé Institute and has taught in over 40 countries.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Capoeira survived slavery by hiding in plain sight — what other living knowledges are we missing because we're only looking in approved places?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Mastery in capoeira and its application to conflict transformation, leadership, and ecological practice. A global network of practitioners in 40 countries. The Axé Institute's curriculum for embodied leadership development.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Communities interested in Afro-Brazilian movement traditions as tools for healing and political organizing. Researchers documenting the wisdom embedded in African diaspora movement forms with genuine respect for their living nature.
elements.label: Carlos Eduardo Mendes

elements.address: 3555 E Patrick Rd, Midland, MI 48642, USA
elements.summary: Rowan Blackfeather is an Anishinaabe educator, traditional knowledge keeper, and land-based learning practitioner from the Great Lakes region who works with indigenous youth and allied communities on reconnecting with ancestral relationships to land, water, and non-human beings. His seasonal land-based programs have served over 2,000 young people across the US and Canada.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Our relationship to land was never broken — it was disrupted. The land still remembers. My work is helping people remember that they have the capacity to remember.
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Land-based seasonal learning program curricula developed over twenty years with 2,000+ youth. Anishinaabe traditional ecological knowledge and protocols for working with land as teacher rather than resource.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Funding and institutional support to scale land-based education without sacrificing indigenous protocols to the demands of scale. Educators from other traditions interested in respectful exchange about land-based pedagogy.
elements.label: Rowan Blackfeather

elements.summary: Alejandro Vega is an urban cooperative developer and social architect from Medellín whose work transforms abandoned industrial sites into worker-owned production hubs and community spaces. Through his organization Cooperativa del Futuro, he has developed over 15 cooperative enterprises employing 800 people and consults with cities across Latin America on cooperative urban development strategies.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: The community enterprise I'm most proud of was built on the site of a factory where workers were exploited for decades. Space carries memory — does economic form carry it too?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: A proven model for transforming abandoned industrial sites into worker-owned enterprises. 800 employed community members as living proof of concept. Consulting expertise for cities experimenting with cooperative urban development.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Cities and municipalities willing to seriously experiment with cooperativism as an urban development strategy. Patient capital willing to finance worker ownership transitions without demanding extraction.
elements.label: Alejandro Vega

elements.address: Cra. 11 N° 82 - 01 Oficina 902, Centro Comercial Andino, Bogotá, Colombia
elements.summary: Valentín Espinoza is a Bogotá-based open-source developer and regenerative tool builder whose work creates digital infrastructure for cooperative enterprises and social movements across Latin America. He is lead developer of ComúnPay, an open-source mutual credit currency system now used by 200 cooperatives across Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: ComúnPay is used by 200 cooperatives. The technology works. The bottleneck now is political will and economic imagination — how do we build those as deliberately as we build software?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: The ComúnPay codebase — an open-source mutual credit currency system. Technical development capacity for cooperative digital infrastructure. A network of 200 cooperatives in Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru as a testing and learning community.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Developers and economists interested in extending ComúnPay to new contexts and scales. Cooperatives in other regions wanting to adapt mutual credit systems and willing to share what they learn.
elements.label: Valentín Espinoza

elements.summary: Kiri Atkinson is a Māori technologist and indigenous data sovereignty researcher from Auckland whose work examines how indigenous communities can exercise control over data about themselves, their land, and their cultural heritage. She is a co-founder of the Te Hiku Media initiative and an advisor to the Global Indigenous Data Alliance.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Our data tells stories about us that we have not consented to. What would true data sovereignty look like — not just for Māori, but for every community that extractive systems have made legible against their will?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Frameworks for indigenous data sovereignty developed through Te Hiku Media. Global Indigenous Data Alliance connections and advocacy capacity. Deep experience navigating the intersection of technology governance and indigenous rights.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Technologists willing to build data systems that genuinely serve community self-determination rather than platform needs. Legal advocates working on data rights for indigenous and historically marginalized communities.
elements.label: Kiri Atkinson

elements.summary: Aditi Bhatt is a learning community designer and social innovator from Ahmedabad whose work creates educational environments that bridge traditional Indian knowledge systems with contemporary design thinking. She is founder of the Vruksha Learning Community and co-developer of Spiral Inquiry, an educational methodology used in over 100 schools across India and Southeast Asia.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: How do we create learning environments that make children wiser rather than just smarter — and how do we defend that distinction in a world obsessed with measurement?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Spiral Inquiry methodology used in 100+ schools across India and Southeast Asia. The Vruksha Learning Community as a living lab and site visit opportunity. Bridge-building between traditional Indian knowledge systems and contemporary design thinking.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Funders and educational reformers willing to invest in models that cannot be easily standardized or scaled without losing their essence. Educators globally working on decolonial and regenerative pedagogies who want genuine peer exchange.
elements.label: Aditi Bhatt

elements.summary: Fatima Al-Rashid is a somatic therapist and trauma educator from Amman, now based in Berlin, specializing in collective and intergenerational trauma in communities affected by displacement and conflict. Her approach, Relational Somatic Healing, integrates body-based therapy with community ritual and has been applied in refugee communities across Europe and the Middle East.
What themes best represent your work?: 3. Good health and well-being for people
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Collective trauma doesn't just live in memory — it lives in the body's default settings. What would it take to change a community's nervous system?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Relational Somatic Healing methodology applied in refugee communities across Europe and the Middle East. Experience integrating body-based therapy with community ritual. Training capacity for practitioners working with collective and intergenerational trauma.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Communities and organizations working with displaced or trauma-affected populations who want somatic tools that go beyond individual therapy. Researchers documenting body-based approaches to collective healing.
elements.label: Fatima Al-Rashid

elements.summary: Sandi MacTavish is a social enterprise developer and systems facilitator from Edinburgh whose practice focuses on building resilient economic ecosystems in post-industrial communities. She founded the Clyde Commons initiative, which has launched 30 community enterprises and a £12 million community wealth fund in Glasgow and surrounding boroughs.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: The Clyde Commons didn't just create jobs — it created people who believed in each other. How do we measure that, and how do we replicate it somewhere entirely different?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: A proven model for community wealth building in post-industrial contexts. A £12 million community wealth fund as detailed case study. Thirty community enterprises as a peer learning network for others starting out.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Post-industrial communities in transition interested in cooperative economic development. Patient capital investors willing to back community wealth initiatives with genuinely long time horizons and no extraction imperative.
elements.label: Sandi MacTavish

elements.summary: Tomasz Kowalski is a Systemic Constellation facilitator and organizational ecologist from Warsaw, now based in Berlin, who works with complex organizations navigating transitions — mergers, culture shifts, and leadership succession. Drawing on the work of Bert Hellinger and complexity science, he helps systems see and work with the hidden orders that govern collective behavior.
What themes best represent your work?: 3. Good health and well-being for people
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Every organization I work with has hidden orders — loyalties, exclusions, debts — that are shaping it without anyone knowing. What would it mean to make those visible as a normal governance practice?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Systemic Constellation methodology for organizations navigating complex transitions. Twenty years of facilitation experience across mergers, culture shifts, and leadership succession. A bridge between Bert Hellinger's work and contemporary complexity science.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Organizations in deep transition willing to engage with the hidden systemic orders governing them — not as a spiritual exercise, but as a rigorous organizational practice. Complexity researchers interested in the empirical evidence base for constellation work.
elements.label: Tomasz Kowalski

elements.summary: Nkechi Okonkwo is a commons practitioner and digital rights activist based in Nairobi working at the intersection of data sovereignty, community governance, and regenerative technology. As a fellow at Gaia Collective, she researches how African commons traditions can inform the design of digital infrastructure that serves community self-determination.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: African commons traditions have governed shared resources for centuries without Elinor Ostrom. What happens when the researchers become the students?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Research on African digital commons and data sovereignty. Connection to practitioner networks across East Africa. Frameworks for translating traditional commons governance into digital infrastructure design.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Technology developers willing to be genuinely accountable to African communities rather than extracting from them. Funders investing in community-governed digital infrastructure in the Global South.
elements.label: Nkechi Okonkwo

elements.summary: Jacques Beaumont is a dialogue practitioner and organizational consultant from Montreal, splitting his time with Paris, who has spent 25 years developing conversation practices for communities in conflict. Trained in Bohmian dialogue, Forum Theatre, and indigenous talking circle protocols, he designs multi-stakeholder processes for municipalities, movements, and international institutions.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: What would it take for a genuine conversation — one where people actually change — to be considered a political act worth funding and organizing around?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Twenty-five years of conversation practice design across conflict zones and contested communities. Fluency in Bohmian dialogue, Forum Theatre, and talking circle protocols. Training capacity for facilitators working in deeply polarized contexts.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Municipal governments and social movements that want to embed genuine dialogue processes — not consultations — into governance structures. Funders willing to support long-duration conversation work.
elements.label: Jacques Beaumont

elements.summary: Oluwatobi Adewale is a food systems organizer and urban farmer from Ibadan whose work builds food sovereignty infrastructure in Nigerian cities through networks of community gardens, seed banks, and food cooperatives. A Bioneers fellow, he connects urban food work to broader questions of ecological sovereignty and intergenerational knowledge transmission.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Seed sovereignty is political sovereignty. When do we start treating it that way in our organizing frameworks?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: A working model for urban food sovereignty infrastructure: community gardens, seed banks, and food cooperatives linked into a resilient network across Nigerian cities. Organizing frameworks connecting urban food work to broader ecological and political sovereignty.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Funders willing to invest in the unglamorous permanent infrastructure of food sovereignty — not just pilot projects. Urban farmers across West Africa interested in building a regional solidarity network.
elements.label: Oluwatobi Adewale

elements.summary: Guadalupe Flores is a community organizer and indigenous rights activist from Oaxaca whose work centers Zapotec principles of communal governance — tequio, cargo, and guelaguetza — as living alternatives to capitalist organization. She coordinates a network of 60 indigenous cooperatives across Oaxaca and leads delegations to international forums on the rights of nature.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: How do we protect indigenous governance models from being co-opted into the language of "alternatives" when they have never stopped being the living center of our communities?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Twenty years of experience organizing with indigenous cooperatives across Oaxaca. Grounded insight into how Zapotec governance principles — tequio, cargo, guelaguetza — function as living economic and political models that predate and outlast the state.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Legal advocates experienced in rights-of-nature frameworks. International scholars working respectfully with indigenous governance who want genuine exchange rather than extraction.
elements.label: Guadalupe Flores

elements.summary: Miriam Goldstein is a cooperative finance practitioner and economic democracy advocate from Jerusalem, now based in New York City, whose work develops patient capital models for worker cooperatives and community land trusts. She is a co-founder of the Shared Equity Lending Collaborative and a board member of the US Federation of Worker Cooperatives.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Finance has been used as a weapon against cooperative enterprise for two hundred years. What would it mean to build financial institutions with genuinely different values in their DNA?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Deep expertise in patient capital models for worker cooperatives and community land trusts. Network connections across cooperative finance practitioners in North America and Europe. Capacity to structure deals that genuinely serve democratic ownership.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Worker cooperatives and community land trusts seeking long-term capital partners who share their values. Wealthy individuals looking to align assets with democratic ownership and community benefit.
elements.label: Miriam Goldstein

elements.summary: Nadia Osei is a participatory design facilitator and community researcher from Accra, now based in Amsterdam, who specializes in co-design processes for communities historically excluded from the design of their own built and digital environments. She is a faculty member at the Amsterdam University of the Arts and a co-founder of the African Design Network.
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: When communities design for themselves, the results are more beautiful and more resilient than anything a professional could produce alone. Why is this still considered a radical claim?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: Co-design methodology developed specifically for communities historically excluded from design processes. Faculty connections at Amsterdam University of the Arts. The African Design Network's practitioner community across the continent and diaspora.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Communities — especially African and Afro-diasporic — who want to design their own digital and physical environments and need a skilled facilitator. Design schools genuinely committed to decolonizing their pedagogies.
elements.label: Nadia Osei

elements.summary: Kameko Ishida is a Butoh practitioner and embodied ecology educator from Kyoto who uses slow, metamorphic movement to explore the body's relationship with the more-than-human world. She teaches in Japan, Europe, and North America and is developing a practice framework called 'Soil Body' that works with decomposition and regeneration as choreographic principles.
What themes best represent your work?: 3. Good health and well-being for people
elements.What's a question you're sitting with th: Decomposition is the most creative process in nature. What would it mean to let some of our institutions fully decompose before we build what comes next?
elements.What can you offer to this community?: The Soil Body practice framework — working with decomposition and regeneration as choreographic principles. Butoh training and its application to ecological education. A method for slowing enough to let endings complete themselves.
elements.What are you looking for most right now?: Ecologists and land stewards interested in slow movement practice as a form of ecological attunement. Institutions in transition willing to explore what needs to fully end before something genuinely new can begin.
elements.label: Kameko Ishida

elements.summary: Drawing on Christopher Alexander's pattern language, indigenous ecological knowledge, and contemporary complexity science, this opening keynote argues that all healthy living systems share a deep grammar — and that learning to read and write in that grammar is the design literacy of our time. What would it mean to design our institutions, our cities, and our relationships as living systems rather than machines?
elements.label: The Pattern Language of Living Systems

elements.summary: How do artists working at the intersection of ecology, social systems, and living matter contribute to collective sense-making? This panel brings together visual artists, choreographers, and sound practitioners to explore art not as illustration but as a primary mode of systems intelligence — a way of perceiving, holding, and transmitting pattern.
elements.label: Art as Systems Literacy: Creative Practice in Complex Times

elements.summary: An open gathering time in the Exhibition Hall where participants can move freely between installations, connect with other attendees, and explore the Marketplace of Practices — a collection of project and organization tables from participating networks. Coffee, tea, and locally sourced food provided.
elements.label: Morning Reception & Marketplace

elements.summary: A ritual opening that invites participants into the shared field of the gathering. Drawing on Indigenous welcome protocols from the Chinook people on whose land we gather, spoken word from Kwame Asante, and communal singing led by Saoirse McCarthy, this ceremony marks the threshold from ordinary time into gathering time.
elements.label: Opening Ceremony: Arriving Together

elements.summary: A panel of practitioners experimenting with non-hierarchical governance in organizations, cooperatives, and municipalities. Panelists will share specific mechanisms, failures, and emergent successes — and interrogate what fungal, watershed, and forest governance actually offers to human institutions trying to move beyond command-and-control.
elements.label: Governing Like a Forest: Decentralized Decision-Making in Practice

elements.summary: This keynote invites a reckoning with what Western systems science is only beginning to articulate — that the land, water, and living beings of a place are intelligent systems that have been self-organizing, self-healing, and communicating for millions of years. Grounding the talk in Cherokee ecological practice and contemporary complexity science, this talk proposes indigenous land stewardship as the original systems design.
elements.label: What the Land Knows: Indigenous Ecological Intelligence in the Age of Complexity

elements.summary: A festive closing of the day's formal programming. Live music from Amir Hassan, movement led by Valentina Cruz, and a short reflection from the day's threads woven together by Elena Volkov. The event then opens into an evening reception where the conversation continues informally.
elements.label: Evening Celebration: The Pattern Holds

elements.summary: A provocative and practical keynote on what it means to design with living systems rather than for them. Drawing from 20 years of practice at the intersection of biomimicry, participatory design, and complexity science, this talk reframes the designer's role from solver to cultivator — someone who creates conditions for self-organization rather than imposing predetermined solutions.
elements.label: Designing for Emergence: From Control to Participation

elements.summary: A contemplative closing ritual facilitated by Rowan Blackfeather and Tomasz Kowalski. Participants are invited to name one thread they are carrying forward from the day, offered into a collective weaving. The ceremony closes with a song of thanks and a commitment to continued meeting.
elements.label: Closing Ceremony: Carrying It Forward

elements.summary: Fungal networks offer one of nature's most radical models of distributed intelligence, resource sharing, and transformation through decomposition. This keynote by mycologist Dr. Abebe Tadesse draws on field research from African forest ecosystems to offer a new vocabulary for thinking about networks, regeneration, and the creative power of breakdown. Co-presented with an original sound composition by Amir Hassan.
elements.label: The Mycelial Model: Networks, Decay, and Renewal

elements.summary: A hands-on workshop in causal loop diagramming — the core tool of systems dynamics for mapping feedback relationships in complex systems. Participants will learn to identify reinforcing and balancing loops, explore leverage points, and apply the methodology to a social or ecological challenge of their choosing. No prior systems modeling experience required.
elements.label: Causal Loop Diagrams: Making the Invisible Visible

elements.summary: Scientists, artists, and practitioners who work at the edge of Western science and other knowledge traditions share their experience of inhabiting the threshold — where empirical research meets ancestral knowing, and where the edges of disciplines become the most interesting places to stand.
elements.label: Where Science Meets Spirit: Navigating the Liminal in Research and Practice

elements.summary: What would finance look like if it were designed for regeneration rather than extraction? Panelists from cooperative finance, community land trusts, and mutual credit systems discuss real mechanisms for circulating resources in ways that build ecological and social wealth. Includes case studies from Latin America, East Africa, and the US.
elements.label: The Economics of Enough: Regenerative Finance and the Commons

elements.summary: This workshop proposes that organizational transformation is always also bodily transformation — that stuck patterns in institutions are held in the bodies of the people within them. Drawing on somatic therapy, movement practice, and systems thinking, participants will explore how embodied practice can unlock organizational change.
elements.label: Somatic Systems: Embodied Approaches to Organizational Change

elements.summary: A frank conversation among practitioners who have navigated the terrain between extractive systems and regenerative alternatives. Panelists will share the texture of transitions they have witnessed or led — what breaks down, what emerges, and what practices sustain people through the in-between. Facilitated dialogue format.
elements.label: From Extraction to Restoration: Practitioner Reflections on Transition

elements.summary: A writing and movement workshop that uses the composting process as a metaphor and practice for processing what needs to die in order for something new to emerge. Participants will write, share, and move through personal and collective grief, guided by somatic and narrative practices rooted in ecological cycles.
elements.label: Composting the Old Story: Writing Through Grief and Transformation

elements.summary: The Living Building Challenge sets the world's highest standard for regenerative building — structures that give more than they take, generate their own energy and water, and celebrate the culture and ecology of their place. This virtual workshop introduces practitioners to the framework and explores its application beyond buildings to communities and systems.
elements.label: Living Building Challenge: Designing Regenerative Structures

elements.summary: An experiential workshop applying the Theory U framework to real community challenges brought by participants. Drawing on the Presencing Institute's methodology, facilitators guide participants through the U-process — from downloading current patterns to sensing emerging possibilities to crystallizing new action. Participants leave with a personal map and a committed next step.
elements.label: Mapping the Living System: Theory U Applied to Community Change
